. Rather than remembering long, tool-specific flags, everyone uses the same vocabulary:
. Understanding how scripts work under the hood lets you write them confidently rather than just copying them from boilerplate.

Exits with the command's exit code. A non-zero exit code means the script failed (CI will mark the run as failed).

npm run dev       # starts the Vite dev server
npm run build     # type-checks then bundles
npm run test      # runs vitest
npm test          # alias — no 'run' needed for 'test'
npm start         # alias — no 'run' needed for 'start'
and runs them before and after. If the main script fails, the
hook above prevents installing with pnpm if the project expects npm — a common enforcement pattern in teams. The globally-recognised lifecycle hooks (regardless of user-defined scripts) include:
Set up Husky git hooks after install
Run tests and build before publishing a library
Script values are plain shell strings. You can use standard shell operators to compose commands:
work on macOS, Linux, and modern Windows (PowerShell 7+). The
are Unix-only. If your team includes Windows developers, use

# Run vitest but only test files matching 'auth':
npm run test -- auth
# expands to: vitest auth

# Run build with a custom base URL:
npm run build -- --base=/app/
# expands to: tsc && vite build --base=/app/

# Pass multiple flags:
npm run lint -- --fix --max-warnings 0
itself, not passed to the script.
npm sets several environment variables automatically when a script runs. You can also set your own inline:
npm also exposes package.json fields as environment variables prefixed

# In a script, you can read:
echo $npm_package_name     # "my-react-app"
echo $npm_package_version  # "0.1.0"
echo $npm_lifecycle_event  # "build" (the script name that was run)

# Useful in Node.js scripts called by npm:
process.env.npm_package_version // '0.1.0'

Running Scripts in Parallel with concurrently
During development, you often need to run multiple watchers simultaneously — the Vite dev server, a TypeScript type-checker in watch mode, and maybe a mock API server. The
assigns colours to each process in the terminal output. If any process exits with a non-zero code,
can be configured to kill the others (
The community has converged on a standard set of script names. Sticking to these conventions means anyone who clones your repo can immediately run it without reading docs:
npm run typecheck && npm run lint && npm run test:ci && npm run build
Scripts don't have to invoke CLI tools — they can run custom Node.js scripts directly. This is useful for codegen, file manipulation, or one-off tasks:
Custom scripts have access to the full Node.js API and all installed packages. They run in the same environment as the rest of your tooling — no separate build step or bundling needed.
without a local install) will fail on machines where the global isn't present. Always install tools as
— if a script swallows exit codes (e.g.,
), CI will show green even on failure. Only suppress exit codes intentionally and document why.
scripts that run when you install them. This is how Husky sets up git hooks. Be aware they run arbitrary code — only install packages you trust.
When you join a new project, the first thing to do is check what scripts are available:

# List all scripts defined in package.json:
npm run
# Output:
#   dev
#     vite
#   build
#     tsc && vite build
#   test
#     vitest
#   ...

# Show the scripts field only:
cat package.json | grep -A 20 '"scripts"'
with no arguments prints every defined script and its command. This is the fastest way to understand what a project offers without reading documentation.
Inside any running script, npm sets
to the name of the script that triggered the current process. This lets a single Node.js script behave differently depending on how it was called:
Git Hooks with Husky and lint-staged
The most common use of the
lifecycle hook is setting up Husky — a tool that installs git hooks so ESLint and Prettier run automatically on every commit:

npm install -D husky lint-staged
npx husky init
# creates .husky/pre-commit

# .husky/pre-commit — runs before every git commit:
npx lint-staged
With this setup: when any developer runs
hook runs Husky, which installs the git pre-commit hook. From then on, every
automatically lints and formats staged files before the commit is recorded.
Writing a Complete CI-Ready scripts Block
Here is a production-quality scripts configuration for a TypeScript React project, including all the pieces discussed above:

script is the most important one for a CI pipeline — it runs every check gate in sequence and fails the build if any step returns a non-zero exit code. GitHub Actions (and most CI platforms) treat a non-zero exit code as a build failure, so this one command gates merges.
In a monorepo using npm workspaces, you can run a script in all workspaces at once from the root:

# Run 'build' in every workspace:
npm run build --workspaces

# Run 'test' only in the 'apps/web' workspace:
npm run test --workspace apps/web

# Run 'build' only in workspaces that have that script:
npm run build --workspaces --if-present
flag is important: without it, npm errors if a workspace does not have the named script. With it, the command silently skips workspaces that don't define the script.
Vite's CLI supports several flags you will commonly pass via npm scripts:
flag exposes the dev server on the local network — useful for testing on mobile devices. Without it, the server is only accessible from
Vitest offers several modes through the CLI that map well to different use cases:
in CI (exits after one pass) and
locally (watch mode). This is a common distinction across test runners.
Vite exposes environment variables to your React code through
are exposed to the browser bundle (to avoid accidentally leaking secrets):

# .env (loaded for all modes)
VITE_API_URL=https://api.myapp.com
VITE_APP_TITLE=MyApp
SECRET_KEY=never-exposed  # no VITE_ prefix — stays server-side

# .env.development (loaded only with npm run dev)
VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:4000

# .env.production (loaded with npm run build)
VITE_API_URL=https://api.myapp.com
files are NOT committed if they contain secrets — add
file with non-secret defaults can be committed.
Running Arbitrary CLI Tools with npx
inside scripts to run tools that are not installed as dependencies:

in scripts has a subtle downside: the first run downloads the package from npm, which is slower than using a locally-installed version and fails in offline environments. Prefer pinning frequently-used tools in

npm run a && npm run b
for running multiple processes in parallel.
